segments.s057-g137: فرود آمد is a compound past tense ('فرود' + آمدن) meaning 'he descended/alighted' (آمد is 3rd-singular past). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. تخت را is the definite direct object ('تخت' = 'the throne' + را object marker). داد بوس is a light-verb construction: داد ('gave') + the noun بوس ('a kiss'), so تخت را داد بوس = 'he gave the throne a kiss' i.e. 'he kissed the throne.' Word order places the verb phrase after فرود آمد but the whole clause reads smoothly as two coordinated past actions by the same subject.
segments.s057-g138: ببستند is 3rd-person-plural past ('they fastened/tied/established'); the grammatical subject is omitted and understood as the crowd/attendants. بر is the preposition 'on/upon'. کوهه is a poetic form of کوه 'mountain'. پیل here is figurative (modifying کوس) meaning 'giant/elephant-like', and کوس is 'war-drum'; together پیل کوس describes a very large drum. The sequence means 'they set/fastened a huge war-drum upon the mountain.'
segments.s057-g139: سوی is a preposition meaning 'toward' placed before the place name زابلستان. نهادند is the 3rd-person plural past of نهادن ('to place/set'); here it combines with روی ('face') as its object in the pattern 'نهادنِ روی' = 'to set/turn (one's) face (toward)'. Word order is Preposition + Place + Verb + Object (poetic), so literally 'they set (their) face toward Zabulistan'.
segments.s057-g140: نظاره is a noun 'gaze/sight'. The phrase uses two برs: بر او ('upon him') marks the target of the gaze, and the following بر introduces the scope/location ('over/throughout') همه شهر و کوی ('every city and quarter'). There is no expressed finite verb here (a common poetic ellipsis); the whole phrase reads as a nominal statement roughly '(there was) a gaze upon him throughout every town and quarter' i.e. 'everywhere people looked at him.'
segments.s057-g141: چو is the archaic/poetic subordinating particle 'when'. آمد is the 3rd-person singular past of آمدن ('he came'). به نزدیکی is a prepositional phrase 'to the vicinity/near(ness) of', and نیمروز here is a place-name (Nimruz) rather than 'noon' — together: 'When he came near Nimruz.'
segments.s057-g142: خبر شد is an impersonal/verbless-construction-like past: شد ('it became'/happened) with خبر ('news') = 'news arrived/it became known'. ز is the poetic preposition 'about/from' introducing the source/content of the news. سالار is the noun 'commander/chief' and گیتی فروز is an epithet 'world-illuminating' (adjective phrase following the noun, a common Persian order), so the whole clause: 'news came about the world-illuminating commander.'
segments.s057-g143: بیاراسته here functions poetically to mean 'adorned/beautified' (likely a contracted/poetic form corresponding to به‌آراسته); it modifies سیستان, the place. چون introduces a simile 'like/as', followed by بهشت 'paradise' — 'Sistan adorned like paradise.' Note: the lemma list gave only negative senses for بیاراسته, but in context the word is used positively (poetic variant).
segments.s057-g144: گلش is گل + ـش (possessive) 'its flowers'. مشک سارا is a compound adjective meaning 'musk‑suffused / giving off musk' (سارا here functions as part of a descriptive compound rather than a proper name). بد is an archaic poetic form corresponding to بود ('was') — a past copula — linking the predicate to گلش. و زر خشت coordinates the next image: زر 'gold' modifying خشت 'brick/block' (i.e. 'gold bricks'). The whole clause: 'its flowers were (perfumed with) musk, and (its) bricks were of gold.'
segments.s057-g33: منوچهر is the subject (proper name). برگاه بنشست is a compact past‑tense clause: برگاه (on the throne / at court) + بنشست (3rd person singular past of نشستن) = 'sat on the throne'. شاد is a predicative adverb/adjective after the verb giving his state 'joyful'. The word order (subject → place→verb →state) is typical of Ferdowsi's poetic, slightly inverted syntax.
segments.s057-g34: کلاه بزرگی is a noun phrase where بزرگی (here 'large/ of large size') modifies کلاه, yielding 'a large hat' (indefinite). به سر is the locative phrase 'upon the head'. برنهاد is a literary/past form meaning 'placed on' or 'put on' (3rd person singular past); together the segment means 'he put a large hat on his head'.
segments.s057-g35: به یک دست ... به یک دست ... is a spatial phrase 'on one hand/on one side ... on the other hand/on the other side ...' where به + یک دست marks the side; the two names (قارن, سام) occupy those positions. The prepositional repetition emphasizes the paired arrangement at the throne.
segments.s057-g36: نشستند is the 3rd person plural past of نشستن, 'they sat'. روشن‌دل و شادکام are two postpositive adjectives/adverbial states describing them: روشن‌دل 'bright‑minded/clear‑hearted' and شادکام 'fortunate/joyful'. The verb applies to both named persons and the adjectives characterize their disposition while sitting.
segments.s057-g37: پس is a temporal/conclusive adverb 'then'. آراسته modifies زال ('the adorned Zal'); را is the definite object marker, so آراسته زال را = 'the adorned Zal (as direct object)'. پیش شاه is the prepositional phrase 'before the king / in the king's presence' indicating to whom he was presented.
segments.s057-g38: This lists the accoutrements using repeated به 'with/at' phrases: به زرین عمود = 'with a golden column' (زرین = golden; عمود = column/pillar). و به رزین کلاه = 'and with a resinous/varnished hat' (رزین here indicates resin/varnish applied to the hat). The preposition به attaches each instrument/ornament to the presentation.
segments.s057-g39: This segment uses a poetic word order: گرازان (boars) appears before the verb and combines with بار ('a load') to mean 'a load of boars'. بیاورد is the 3rd singular past 'brought/produced'; سالار is the agent 'the chief/lord' — here the subject. The overall sense is 'the lord brought a load (of) boars.'
segments.s057-g40: شگفتی بماند اندر او شهریار is a verbless/nominal clause common in epic poetry: شگفتی 'astonishment' + بماند (3rd sg. present/past‑stative) = 'remained astonishment'; اندر او 'within him' specifies the experiencer, and شهریار clarifies that 'he' is the king. The phrase means 'astonishment remained in him, the king' (i.e., the king was amazed).
segments.s057-g65: همی is an adverbial particle here marking continuous or habitual aspect ('continually/indeed'); بوی مهر is a noun phrase 'the scent of affection' (noun + genitive), followed by آمد 'came' (past tense verb). از باد اوی is a prepositional phrase 'from the wind/breeze of him/her' where اوی is an older possessive form ('his/her'). Word order is noun + verb then the source phrase.
segments.s057-g66: به دل is a prepositional phrase 'to/into the heart.' راحت functions as a noun/adjective meaning 'ease/comfort' that has 'آمد' ('came') as its verb, so 'ease came to the heart.' هم 'also' links this clause to the previous one. از یاد اوی is another source phrase 'from the remembrance of him/her,' with اوی again the poetic/archaic possessive.
segments.s057-g67: ابا (literally 'reluctance/refusal') here is used idiomatically to indicate that the speaker had no reluctance (a poetic ellipsis). داور ('the Judge') is the addressee; راست گفتم is the verb phrase 'I spoke the truth' — گفتم is past tense 'I said' and راست qualifies it as truthful. به راز is an adverbial phrase 'in secret' (preposition + noun).
segments.s057-g68: که‌ای here functions as a vocative/introducer addressing the addressee ('O …'); آفریننده is the noun 'Creator,' and بی‌نیاز is an adjective 'self-sufficient' modifying it. The phrase is a direct address: 'O self-sufficient Creator.'
segments.s057-g69: رسیده is a perfect participle/predicate 'has reached' or 'reached'; به هر جای is the prepositional phrase 'to every place.' برهان تو ('your proof') is the subject/possessive noun phrase. Poetic word order places the predicate before the subject and location.
segments.s057-g70: نگردد is the negative present/subjunctive form meaning 'does not turn/move' (here a general statement about the heavens). فلک is the subject 'the sky/heaven.' جز به فرمان تو is an exception phrase 'except by your command,' where جز works as 'except' and به فرمان تو is 'by/under your command.'
segments.s057-g71: یکی is an indefinite/qualifying word 'a certain' or 'one' used modestly; بنده‌ام is a humble self-reference 'I am (your) servant' with the first-person suffix. با تنی پر گناه is a prepositional phrase 'with a body full of sin' (تنی = bodily/corporeal, پر = 'full of', گناه = 'sin').
segments.s057-g72: به پیش is a prepositional phrase meaning 'before' or 'in the presence of.' خداوند خورشید و ماه is a title-form noun phrase 'the Lord (sovereign) of the sun and moon' — خداوند used as a sovereign/title followed by the objects خورشید و ماه.
segments.s057-g1: یکایک is an adverb meaning “one by one” or “one after another,” here modifying آمد. به شاه آمد is a prepositional phrase (to the king) placed before the verb آمد (past ‘came/reached’), and این آگهی is the topical noun phrase ‘this news/information’ that is presented as what arrived. The overall order is adverb → indirect object → verb → topical noun, a common flexible poetic word order in Persian.
segments.s057-g2: که is the conjunction ‘that’ introducing reported content. سام آمد is a simple past clause ‘Sam came.’ از کوه is the source phrase ‘from the mountain.’ با فرهی is a manner phrase formed with با + فرهی, where فرهی is used poetically as a quality (‘noble / with dignity’); together it gives the sense ‘with nobility / in noble guise.’
segments.s057-g3: بدان here is the fused expression (به + آن) meaning ‘upon that / at that.’ آگهی شد literally ‘the news became known,’ but the whole phrase functions adverbially: ‘on that news.’ منوچهر شاد is the main clause with subject (Manuchehr) followed by the predicate adjective شاد ‘happy/joyful’; شد is the past copular/become verb linking the state to the subject.
segments.s057-g4: بسی is an intensifying adverb ‘much/very’ qualifying the following remembrance. از marks the object of recollection (‘of/regarding’), جهان‌آفرین is the compound noun ‘world‑creator’ (the Creator), and کرد یاد is a light-verb construction where کرد (to do/make) combines with یاد (remembrance) to mean ‘made remembrance of’ → ‘recalled/remembered.’ The verb کرد carries tense/person as the main verb of the clause.
segments.s057-g5: بفرمود is a polite/honorific past-tense verb ‘he commanded/ordered/said.’ تا introduces a subordinate clause of result/purpose meaning ‘that/so that.’ نوذر نامدار is a nominal phrase (Nowzar + the adjective 名دار ‘renowned’) placed before the clause-final verb; the subordinate clause is not complete until the verb in the next segment.
segments.s057-g6: شود is the subjunctive/passive-form of شدن ‘become/be made’ required after تا for an intended/result clause (‘that X be made’). تازیان is the subject of that clause (‘the Arabs’). پیش سام is the locative/prepositional phrase ‘before/in front of Sam,’ and سوار here functions as a predicative adjective ‘mounted’ (i.e., ‘that the Arabs be mounted/in saddle before Sam’).
segments.s057-g7: کند is a present/habitual-narrative form of ‘do/make’ used here to report the action ‘(they) made/gave.’ آفرین is the noun ‘praise/acclamation,’ and کیانی qualifies it as ‘Kayanian’ (royal/Kayanian). بر اوی is the prepositional phrase ‘upon him/for him’ (the indirect object receiving the praise). The verb is generic/presentive narration rather than a first/second person command.
segments.s057-g8: بدان is again the fused prepositional phrase (به + آن) meaning ‘with that’ or ‘at that,’ here linking to شادمانی ‘joy.’ که is the conjunction ‘that’ introducing a result clause. بگشاد is a poetic/archaic verbal form meaning ‘opened’ (here ‘his face opened’ i.e. it brightened or he smiled), where the prefix ب- is an older verbal prefix; روی is the noun ‘face.’ The clause thus reads ‘with that joy, that his face opened’ → ‘with such joy that his face brightened.’
segments.s057-g9: بفرمایدش is a third-person narrative/optative form meaning “he commanded/ordered him” with the clitic -ش = “him”. تا introduces purpose/endpoint here: “in order that / to”. سوی is a preposition meaning “toward/to”. شهریار is the addressee noun “the king.” Together: “He ordered him to go toward the king.”
segments.s057-g10: شود is the narrative/subjunctive auxiliary meaning here “may/become” (used to mark purpose/result). تا again marks purpose “so that.” سخن‌ها is the plural “words/speeches.” کند is the present/subjunctive of کردن “to do/make” and pairs with سخن‌ها to mean “to utter/make speeches.” خواستار is used as the object/predicate “petitioner” (one who asks) — together: “so that he might utter his petitions.”
segments.s057-g11: ببیند is a narrative/past-form often used in epic prose/poetry meaning “he saw” (this present-subjunctive shape serves as past narration). یکی is the indefinite “a certain / one.” روی here is the noun “face.” دستان is the genitive/possessive plural “hands (of).” سام is the proper name. Word order is noun phrase after the verb (object-like sequence): “he saw a certain face of Sam’s hands.”
segments.s057-g12: به دیدار = “at / on the meeting” (به + noun used adverbially). ایشان is the polite/plural pronoun “them/their.” شود is the past/narrative “became.” شادکام is an adjective meaning “joyful/fortunate.” The clause reads literally “at meeting them he became joyful.”
segments.s057-g13: و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” زین جا is a poetic locution meaning “from this place / hence” (زین here is a demonstrative element paired with جا “place”); in this edition the element زین has no matching sense entry, so treat it as the demonstrative part of زین‌جا. سوی = “toward/to.” زابلستان is the place-name “Zabulistan.” شود is the narrative auxiliary “became/was directed” used to indicate movement or change of state (“he went / he was directed toward Zabulistan”).
segments.s057-g14: بر آیین = “upon/according to the custom/practice of.” خسرو names the royal figure “Khosrow” (used here as the owner of the named custom). پرستان is a plural noun “worshippers/ devotees.” شود is again the narrative “became” — together: “he became (an adherent) according to the custom of Khosrow’s worshippers,” i.e. he adopted their ways.
segments.s057-g15: چو is the subordinating conjunction “when.” نوذر is the proper name Nowzar. بر before سام functions as the preposition “toward/to/at” (classical usage: “upon Sam / at Sam’s place”). نیرم in the supplied senses would mean “soft/weak,” but that sense does not fit here (this item is likely a textual/place-name or variant and so is marked unknown). رسید is the past “arrived/reached.” Together: “When Nowzar reached (or came to) Sam …”
segments.s057-g16: یکی = “a certain / one.” نو is the adjective “new.” جهان‌پهلوان is a compound noun “world‑champion / mighty hero.” را is the direct‑object marker. بدید is the narrative past “he saw.” The segment literally: “he saw a certain new world‑champion.”
segments.s057-g97: This is a subject phrase: ستاره‌شناسان (star‑readers) is the agent. هم (also/too) follows it for emphasis. اندر زمان is an archaic/prepositional phrase ('in/at the time') with اندر before زمان — a typical Old/epic Persian order.
segments.s057-g98: از اختر (from a/the star) is a prepositional object placed before the verb; گرفتند is a past plural verb 'they took/received'. پیدا is an adjective 'manifest/clear' qualifying نشان 'sign' (پیدا نشان = clear sign). The whole clause reads literally 'from the star they took a clear sign.'
segments.s057-g99: بگفتند is the past plural 'they said/spoke'. با here introduces the addressee ('to/with the king'); شاه is the head noun and دیهیم دار is an epithet attached to شاه (دیهیم 'rural' + دار 'holder/possessor' — an archaic compound describing the king).
segments.s057-g100: که introduces the content/wish of what was said ('that' / optative content). شادان is an adjective 'joyful'; بزی here is an optative/subjunctive verbal form 'may you live' (classical Persian verbal subjunctive), not the noun 'goat' — the provided nominal senses do not cover this verbal use. تا بود روزگار is a temporal clause 'until/as long as time exists' with تا 'until' and بود as the copula/verb 'be'.
segments.s057-g101: که here is the relative/complementizer 'for/that/who' linking this clause; او is the subject 'he'. پهلوانی functions as a predicate noun 'a champion/hero' and بود is the past copula 'was'; نامدار is a postpositive adjective 'renowned'.
segments.s057-g102: This is a string of epithets joined by و: سرافراز (lofty/honored), هشیار (alert/sober), گرد (an archaic/poetic epithet whose ordinary glosses here are not directly applicable and functions as a terse quality), and سوار (horseman/mounted). In epic style adjectives follow or stand in apposition to the predicate.
segments.s057-g103: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; بشنید is the past tense 'he heard'. In poetic word order the verb appears before its noun (بشنید شاه). این سخن is the object 'this speech', and شاد شد is a compound predicate 'became glad/was made happy' (شاد + شد).
segments.s057-g104: دل پهلوان (the hero's heart) is the subject phrase (possessive order: 'heart of the champion'). از غم آزاد شد is the predicate 'became free from sorrow' with از introducing the thing freed from, آزاد as predicate adjective, and شد the past copula 'became'.
segments.s057-g153: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. بر here marks the target of the acclamation and functions like English 'to' (i.e. 'to the hero'). آفرین is an interjection meaning an acclamation ('bravo') and خواندند is a past plural verb 'they cried/sang' (regular past third-person plural) — together 'چو بر پهلوان آفرین خواندند' = 'when they cried "bravo" to the hero'.
segments.s057-g154: This clause is independent and uses the juxtaposition 'ابر زال' as a genitive-like phrase 'Zāl's cloud'. افشاندند is the past plural verb 'they scattered/sprinkled', with زر گوهر as a compound direct object 'gold and gems' (word order: subject — object — verb, typical in poetry where noun phrases may precede the verb).
segments.s057-g155: نشست here is a past-tense verb 'he sat' (not the noun 'session'); آنگهی is the temporal adverb 'then/thereupon'. با marks accompaniment 'with', so 'نشست آنگهی سام با زیب و جام' = 'Then Sam sat with (beauty) and a cup.' Note the simple past narrative order: verb — time — subject — accompaniment is permissible in epic-poetic syntax.
segments.s057-g156: همی is an archaic/progressive particle used here to mark repeated or continuous action ('was continually' / habitual). داد is the past 'gave' and takes چیز ('things') as its object. The second همی again marks ongoing action before راند کام: راند is a past verb 'drove/put to flight' and کام is 'desire'; literally 'he gave things and he drove away desire' — the phrase is compact and idiomatic in the poem, so a reader may interpret it as 'he generously gave and satisfied/dispelled desire.'
segments.s057-g157: This is a past copular clause: 'کسی' = 'someone' (indefinite subject) and 'کو' is an archaic connective/relative ('who/that') linking to the predicate. 'به خلعت سزاوار بود' is 'was deserving of the robe' where به marks the beneficiary/standard ('of/for the robe'), سزاوار is the adjective 'worthy/deserving', and بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s057-g158: Two short predicates joined by و: 'خردمند بود' = 'he was wise' and 'جهاندار بود' = 'he was a world-ruler/sovereign'. Each predicate uses بود (past 'was') as the copula; the simple repetition of بود after each predicate is a typical rhetorical/poetic parallelism.
segments.s057-g159: براندازه‌شان is a compound meaning 'according to their measure/rank' ('-شان' is the third-person plural possessive). خلعت is the object 'robe (of honour)' and آراستند is a past plural verb 'they adorned/arrayed' — together 'they adorned robes proportionate to them' (object before verb for emphasis, common in poetry).
segments.s057-g160: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject here. پایه is 'basis/foundation' (here figuratively 'rank/standing'), برتری is 'superiority/preeminence', and خواستند is the past plural 'they wanted/sought'. The clause therefore means 'all sought superiority' (literally 'all sought the basis of superiority').
segments.s057-g73: امیدم (my hope) is the subject (possessed noun with -م). The prepositional phrase به بخشایشِ تو (in your forgiveness) follows to mark where the hope rests. است is the copula ‘is,’ and بس is a short predicate meaning ‘enough’ or ‘that suffices’ (elliptical: ‘that is enough’). Word order: Possessed noun + prepositional complement + copula + predicate.
segments.s057-g74: به چیزی دگر is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘to/for anything else’ (دگر = other). نیستم (I am not) negates the predicate; دسترس functions as the predicate adjective/noun ‘within reach / having access.’ The construction literally reads ‘I am not accessible to any other thing,’ i.e. ‘I have no other recourse.’
segments.s057-g75: تو (you) is the subject; این بنده is a definite noun phrase ‘this servant’ where بنده is a humble self-reference. مرغ پرورده is a modifying phrase (‘a reared/tamed bird’) placed after the noun in poetic apposition, and را is the definite object marker anticipating the verb in the next segment.
segments.s057-g76: به خواری و زاری is an adverbial/causal phrase ‘in humiliation and lamentation’ (به + noun). برآورده is a past participle/verb meaning ‘brought up / provided’ here (passive/result state) and را again marks the definite object; syntactically the verb completes the object marked in the previous segment: ‘you have brought up (him) in humiliation and wailing.’
segments.s057-g77: همی is an archaic/poetic particle marking habitual or continuous action (‘will / is wont to’). پر (feather) precedes the verb پوشد (he wears/puts on) — typical Persian SOV with object before verb. به جای حریر is a prepositional phrase ‘in place of silk / instead of silk.’
segments.s057-g78: مزد گوشت is a noun phrase ‘the wage/reward (is) meat’ where the copula is omitted (a common poetic device). هنگام پستان شیر is a temporal/genitive phrase ‘at the time of the breast’s milk / when at the breast for suckling.’ Together this functions as the predicate: ‘(his) reward is meat at suckling-time.’
segments.s057-g79: به بدمهری (lit. ‘by bad-affection’) expresses cause/instrument ‘by your ill-will.’ من روانم places the possessor (من) before the possessed noun روانم ‘my soul/spirit.’ مسوز is an imperative negative ‘do not burn’ (مسوز = م + سوز, imperative ‘do not burn’ or ‘do not grieve’).
segments.s057-g80: به من = ‘to/for me.’ باز here functions adverbially ‘again / back’ and بخش is an imperative form ‘grant / give’ (poetic imperative of بخشیدن), so به من باز بخش = ‘restore/grant back to me.’ و links clauses; دلم is ‘my heart’ (دل + possessive -م). بر فروز (فروز = infinitive/imperative ‘illumine/kindle’) is a causative/imperative phrase ‘make my heart shine’ — i.e. ‘brighten my heart.’
segments.s057-g169: بماند is an optative/imperative-like present form (بـ + ماند) meaning 'let him remain' or 'may he remain'. The verb precedes the prepositional phrase and the noun (V + PP + NP): به نزد شما 'with you' (به نزد = 'in the presence/of' or 'with'), then این پسر 'this son' is the referent left in your charge.
segments.s057-g170: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. همتای جان است is predicative: همتای (match/counterpart) + جان (here 'life/soul') + است 'is'. The second conjunct و جفت جگر links another predicate: جفت جگر = 'a pair/peer of the jigar (heart/seat of feeling)'. The clause declares the youth's equivalence to the speaker's inner self; the possessive is implied by context.
segments.s057-g171: دل و جانم: a coordinated possessive 'my heart and my soul' (دل = 'heart', جان = 'soul/life', the -م is the 1st-person possessive suffix). ایدر 'here' indicates location. بماند همی repeats the optative/continuative form 'may ... remain' (بماند) with همی as an archaic continuative/emphatic particle. Word order places the possessed nouns before the locative and verb.
segments.s057-g172: This short clause uses a vivid metaphor: مژه 'eyelash' (agent) + خونِ دل 'the blood of the heart' (object/genitive) + برفشاند (3rd pers. sing. present/past-evocative of برفشاندن) 'scatters/sheds' and همی again as a continuative/emphatic particle. The verb precedes the particle; the whole line means the eyelashes will scatter (i.e., shed like) the heart's blood (weeping).
segments.s057-g173: به گاه جوانی 'at the time of youth' (به گاه = 'on the occasion of'); و کند آوری links another temporal clause 'and when you brought/produced' — کند here is the verb 'to do/make' (classical usage) combined with آوری 'you bring/you caused to come' to form a verbal phrase meaning 'you did/you brought'. This sets the temporal background for the admission in the next segment.
segments.s057-g174: یکی 'once/one time' modifies the verb phrase; بیهده 'futile/idle' qualifies داوری 'judgment'. ساختم is 1st-person singular past 'I made' or 'I performed' (ساختم داوری = 'I rendered/judged'). Word order places the modifier before the noun-phrase object and finishes with the verb (S/Adj + NP + V).
segments.s057-g175: This clause is compact and somewhat archaic in word order: پسر 'the son' is the object/topic; داد here is the verb 'gave/entrusted' (not 'justice'); یزدان 'God' is the indirect object/recipient ('gave to God' = 'entrusted to God'); بیانداختم is a prefixed past form meaning 'I cast aside/put away'. Together they express that the speaker gave the son to God / abandoned him.
segments.s057-g176: ز بی‌دانشی 'from/because of ignorance' uses ز in the causal sense 'because of'. ارج 'worth/honor' is the object of نشناختم, the 1st-person past negative 'I did not recognize/esteem' (نشناختم = 'I did not know/recognize'). The clause admits that ignorance prevented recognition of the son's value.
segments.s057-g105: یکی is an indefinite determiner meaning “a certain” and modifies خلعتی (a robe of honour). ساخت is the past‑tense verb “made” (3sg); in this poetic line the subject شاه زمین (“the king of the land/realm”) follows the verb, a common inversion in classical Persian. The whole segment therefore reads “the king of the realm made a certain robe.”
segments.s057-g106: که introduces a relative clause “which/that.” کردند is 3rd‑person plural past of کردن and here functions with the noun/exclamation آفرین: each person “did (utter)” the acclamation. هر کس is the subject “everyone/each person,” بدو is the indirect object “to him,” and آفرین is an exclamation/noun “(bravo) praise.” Literally: “which everyone did (say) ‘afrin’ to him.”
segments.s057-g107: از marks origin/source “from.” اسپان is the plural of اسپ (horses), and the following adjective تازی specifies them as Arabian/Arabian horses; adjective follows the noun. به here begins the specification of what was provided for those horses (lit. “to/with golden …”). زرین is the adjective “golden.” The final word ستام in the given senses list does not match a clear meaning for a horse‑accoutrement, so its precise sense is uncertain in the supplied lexicon (see senses mapping). The whole phrase is parallel listing a gift associated with Arabian horses.
segments.s057-g108: ز (poetic variant of از) again marks source “from.” شمشیر هندی is a compound: شمشیر “sword” qualified by the adjective هندی “Indian.” به زرین نیام means “(with) a golden scabbard” — نیام is “scabbard/sheath.” The construction is parallel to the previous half‑line: from Indian swords (came) golden scabbards.
segments.s057-g109: ز begins another list of sources “from.” The nouns are coordinated by و: دینار “dinar/coin,” خز “fur/velvet,” then again ز + یاقوت “from (the) ruby” and و زر “and gold.” This is a straightforward enumerative series of valuable items provided.
segments.s057-g110: ز again introduces what comes from those riches: گستردنی‌های is a plural noun built from the verbal/adjectival stem گستردن (“to spread”), here meaning “things that are spread out” (i.e. spreads, laid‑out goods or carpets); -های marks plural. بسیار modifies it as “many.” مر is the archaic/object‑form pronoun “to me.” The verb is omitted (nominal sentence), a common poetic device: “there were many spreads to me” or “many spreads were given to me.”
segments.s057-g111: غلامان is the plural noun “servants/pages.” رومی is the adjective “Rûm/Byzantine” following the noun. به + دیبای روم is a prepositional phrase indicating attire or material: دیبا “brocade/silk” qualified by روم “of Rûm (Byzantium).” In older Persian, به can mark what someone is clothed in or furnished with (“in/with brocade of Rûm”).
segments.s057-g112: همه is a quantifier “all/every.” گوهرش and زرش are possessive compounds (گوهر + -ش “his jewel(s)”; زر + -ش “his gold”). The nouns پیکر and بوم function as predicate/nominative complements with the copula omitted (a typical Persian nominal clause): the sense is that his jewels were upon/adorning their bodies (پیکر) and his gold upon their cloth (بوم, here “fabric/cloth/canvas”). The conjunction و links the two parallel predications.
segments.s057-g17: فرود آمد is a past action 'alighted/descended' (literally 'came down'); the following prepositional phrase از باره سام modifies which group came down — 'from Sam's baggage/retinue' — and the final noun سوار names them ('riders/horsemen'). Poetic word order places the place/possessor phrase before the head noun.
segments.s057-g18: گرفتند is simple past plural 'they took/seized'. مر here is an archaic object pronoun (used where modern Persian would have او/مرا); in this context it functions as the object 'him'/'me' (poetic usage). یک‌دگر را کنار literally 'one another (را) aside' — را marks the definite object and کنار indicates motion/location 'to the side' or 'aside'. The clause therefore means they led/took (him) aside, each in turn.
segments.s057-g19: ز ... ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' (equivalent to modern از) and governs شاه and گردان: 'from the king and from the troop(s)'. بپرسید is the past-tense narrative form 'he asked' (the b- prefix occurs commonly in classical Persian narrative). سام at the end is the subject ('Sam asked ...').
segments.s057-g20: This line uses an inverted, poetic order: 'از ایشان' = 'from them' (source), 'بدو' = 'to him' (indirect object), داد = 'gave' (simple past), and نوذر is the agent (Nowzar) who gives پیام 'a message'. So: 'From them, Nowzar gave him a message.'
segments.s057-g21: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is past 'he heard'. پیغام شاه بزرگ is a noun phrase 'the king's great message' (noun + possessor + adjective), with normal modifier order for Persian poetry.
segments.s057-g22: زمین را is the definite object ('the earth' marked by را), followed by the verb ببوسید 'he kissed' (past). The subject سام سترگ follows the verb — a common poetic order — where سترگ is an epithet appended to Sam ('Sam the mighty' / 'Sam, great one').
segments.s057-g23: دوان is a verbal form meaning 'ran/rushed' (poetic participial/finite form), سوی درگاه 'toward the court' gives the direction, and بنهاد روی literally 'placed/set (his) face' — an idiom meaning 'turned his face/took his course' toward the court. Word order places the verb before the subject/phrase as is typical in epic diction.
segments.s057-g24: چنان 'thus/so' modifies the command; کش here combines with بفرمود to indicate an order to fetch or search (کش + بفرمود = 'he commanded to fetch/search'), and دیهیم جوی is the object of that command: دیهیم 'rural/peasant' and جوی 'seek/search for'. So the clause reports he gave the instruction to search for the villagers.
segments.s057-g89: من is the emphatic 1st‑person subject pronoun 'I'. آوردمش is past tense 1sg آوردم with the enclitic object -ش 'him/it' (so 'I brought him/it'). نزد is a preposition meaning 'in the presence of/ before', followed by the title شاه جهان, a compound noun 'king of the world' where جهان postmodifies شاه.
segments.s057-g90: همه is the quantifier 'all' (modifying what follows). آشکاراش is آشکار(ا) with the enclitic -اش ('its/it') and functions here as the notion 'made manifest/that which is revealed' (poetic/compounded formation). کردم is the 1st‑person past of کردن 'I made/did' used causatively ('I caused to be...'). نهان is the adjective 'hidden' placed after the verb phrase; the whole cluster means 'I made all that was hidden manifest.'
segments.s057-g91: بفرمود is an honorific/politeness past 3rd‑person verb meaning 'he commanded/said'. پس is an adverb 'then/afterwards'. The order places the verb before the subject (شاه) for elevated style. با موبدان is a prepositional phrase 'with the mōbeds' indicating accompaniment.
segments.s057-g92: ستاره‌شناسان is a plural noun 'star‑knowers' and in this context reads as 'astrologers/astrologers‑stargazers' rather than the modern narrow 'astronomers'. و is the coordinating 'and'. هم here means 'also/together' emphasizing inclusion. بخردان is an old plural/collective form meaning 'the wise (men)'.
segments.s057-g93: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that/so that' or a relative content clause. جویند is a present (classical style) 3rd‑person plural meaning 'they seek/inquire'. تا here introduces the purpose/result 'in order to/so that' and is followed by the question clause. اختر زال is a genitive construction 'Zal's star' (noun followed by possessor), while چی ست is the interrogative 'what is' (چی 'what' + ست copula) with the copula at the clause end, a typical verb‑final classical order.
segments.s057-g94: بر آن اختر is a prepositional phrase 'on/about that star' (بر + demonstrative آن + noun اختر). از بخت سالار is a genitive/attributive group 'of (his) fate, the master' or 'by fate the lord' (از used in the sense 'of/concerning' plus بخت 'fate' and appositive/epithet سالار 'lord, master'). کیست is the interrogative 'who is' (کی 'who' + ست copula), with the copula attached and placed at the end as usual in classical syntax.
segments.s057-g95: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. گیرد is 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive of گرفتن 'he takes/seizes/ascends' (classical style). بلندی is the noun 'height' or 'loftiness' serving as the object/complement of گیرد (note: the supplied lemma/senses did not match the noun here, so this is a lexical noun use). چه is the interrogative 'what' and خواهد is the modal/future auxiliary 'will/would' (3rd person); بدن is the noun 'body' (here the subject or nominative referent of the question). The construction is somewhat elliptical/poetic: 'When he attains height, what will the body do?'
segments.s057-g96: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle 'continually/indeed' placed before داستان 'the tale/story' (subject). از is the preposition 'about/concerning'. چه is 'what' and خواهد is again the future/modal auxiliary 'will'. زدن is the infinitive 'to strike/do/perform' (classical polyvalent verb); here it means 'to do/perform' (what will be done). The clause literally says 'the tale keeps (turning) on/about what he will do.'
segments.s057-g57: برفتم is a past‑tense first‑person form meaning “I went/I departed”; the initial b‑ is an archaic/poetic prefix found in older Persian narrative. به فرمان = “by/at the command (of)”: به here marks agency or cause (’by’). گیهان خدای = a genitive/nominal phrase “the God/Lord of the World,” with گیهان (world) qualifying خدای (God).
segments.s057-g58: به البرز کوه = a prepositional phrase “to/into Alborz Mountain,” where به + proper name indicates motion toward. اندر آن زشت جای is a descriptive locative: اندر = “in/within,” آن = “that/the,” زشت جای = “ugly place.” Word order is typical older Persian: determiner/adj follows the noun.
segments.s057-g59: یکی کوه دیدم = “I saw a certain/a particular mountain.” یکی before a noun here means “a certain.” سر اندر سحاب = a relative locative “whose head/top was in the clouds,” with سر = summit/top and اندر سحاب = “in the clouds.” Word order is noun + modifier typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s057-g60: سپهری است گفتی = “(it) is heavenly/sky‑like, (as) they say.” سپهری is an adjective meaning “celestial/skyly.” گفتی here is a parenthetical or reported‑speech marker (literally ‘you said’ in Modern Persian, but in older/narrative usage it often functions like English ‘they say’ or ‘it is said’); the supplied dictionary senses do not include this verbal use, so note it as an impersonal/reported formula. ز خارا بر آب = a concise poetic span “from flint/rock down to water,” with ز = “from.”
segments.s057-g61: برو is the imperative “go/enter.” بر + نشیمی functions as a directive/optative: بر is the preposition “on/upon” and نشیمی is a jussive/optative form derived from نشستن meaning “(let someone) sit” or “(let it) be seated”; together here the phrase means roughly “enter and take your seat” or “let (them) sit upon it.” چو کاخ بلند = a simile “like a lofty palace,” with چو = “as/like.”
segments.s057-g62: ز هر سوی بر او بسته راه گزند is a compact older Persian word order meaning “from every side toward it the road of harm was closed.” ز هر سوی = “from every direction/side”; بر او بسته = “closed upon it” (بسته functioning as a predicative adjective/participle); راه گزند = “the path/way of harm.” Note the inverted order where the adjective (بسته) follows the prepositional phrase.
segments.s057-g63: بدو اندرون = a motion phrase “into it / to its interior” (بدو = be‑ + dow/— here ‘to it’); اندرون = “inside.” بچه مرغ و زال = the two nouns “the bird’s chick and Zal,” with بچه مرغ = “bird’s offspring/chick.” The coordinating و simply links the two nouns.
segments.s057-g64: تو گفتی که هستند هر دو همال = “you said (that) both are همال.” تو گفتی is literally “you said,” but in poetic/narrative registers the verb can also function as a reported‑speech formula; که introduces the content clause; هستند is present plural “are”; هر دو = “both.” همال here is predicative and means something like “matched/peers” (literal dictionary senses offered are ‘carrier/porter’ or figurative ‘bearer’ — the exact nuance is idiomatic/older, so it functions here as ‘of the same sort’).
segments.s057-g249: بدین سان is an adverbial phrase 'in this way/thus' (بدین = با + این in older spelling). همی is the classical continuous/iterative particle that makes the following verb ongoing or habitual in narrative. گشت is the past/preterite of گشتن 'to turn/become' so همی گشت = 'kept turning' / 'was turning'. گردان سپهر = 'the revolving heavens/sky' where گردان is an adjective/participle 'revolving' modifying سپهر; the adjective–noun order here is poetic but functions as a single nominal phrase.
segments.s057-g250: ابر سام literally 'a cloud (over) Sam' uses ابر metaphorically (a cloud fell/gathered over Sam). و is the coordinating 'and'. بر is the preposition 'upon/on', taking زال as its object. گسترده is a past participle 'spread/outstretched' used predicatively with مهر 'affection/love' (here مهر = affection), so بر زال گسترده مهر = 'and affection was spread upon Zal' — the verb sense is supplied by the participle (and the earlier گشت can be understood to carry over rhythmically in the poem). The word order is slightly inverted/poetic (participial adjective with noun).
segments.s057-g81: به فرمان یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'by the command of God' with به used to indicate agency/means. چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when', این گفته is a passive construction 'this (that) having been said' (گفته as past participle) and شد is the auxiliary 'was' forming the passive ('was said').
segments.s057-g82: نیایش is the subject 'the prayer'; همانگه is an adverb meaning 'at that moment / immediately'; پذیرفته شد is a passive perfectal phrase 'was accepted' (پذیرفته past participle + شد auxiliary). Word order places the verb phrase at the end, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s057-g83: بزد پر سیمرغ literally 'beat (its) wing the Simorgh' — an inverted, epic word order where the verb (بزد) comes before its object (پر) and subject (سیمرغ). بر شد به ابر is a verbal complex 'بر شدن' meaning 'to rise/ascend'; به ابر marks the destination ('toward/into the cloud').
segments.s057-g84: همی is a classical progressive/emphatic particle indicating continued or immediate action ('was/then'); حلقه زد is a light‑verb collocation meaning 'made a circle / circled' (حلقه = circle, زد = light verb 'to strike/do'); بر سر marks the locus 'over/upon the head of' and مرد گبر specifies the person 'the gabr-man' (a non‑Muslim/Zoroastrian man in epic usage).
segments.s057-g85: ز کوه اندر آمد is a poetic order 'came from the mountain' (ز = from, اندر = in/into used with آمد). چو introduces a simile 'like/as', so چو ابر بهار = 'like a spring cloud.' The clause uses compact epic diction and inverted order common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s057-g86: گرفته is a past participle 'having seized / having taken hold of'; تن زال را is the definite object 'Zal's body' (را marks the object); بر کنار here means 'to the side / aside' (بر as locative 'on/upon' + کنار 'edge/side'). The whole is a participial clause describing what was done to Zal as the bird descended.
segments.s057-g87: به پیش من آورد is 'brought (to) my presence' (به + پیش = 'to before/in the presence of', من = me, آورد = brought). چون دایه‌ای is a simile 'like a nurse' (چون = like/as, دایه‌ای = 'a nurse/wet‑nurse').
segments.s057-g88: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'; در مهر باشد = 'is/has (in) affection' (در = in, مهر = love/affection, باشد as present/subjunctive 'is/should be'); ورا مایه‌ای means 'has for him some provision/resource' (ورا = for him/her, مایه = resource/provision). The clause qualifies the nurse‑like manner of bringing.
segments.s057-g49: پس از is a prepositional phrase meaning “after.” Here پس (followed by از) introduces a temporal/circumstantial phrase: 'پس ازِ کارِ سیمرغ' = 'after the deed/work of the Simorgh.' The coordinate 'و کوه بلند' simply adds 'and the high mountain' (noun + adjective after the noun in normal Persian order).
segments.s057-g50: و زان تا ... is a temporal/causal continuation: زان ('from that') + تا ('up to/ever since') marks the ensuing time span. چرا here is an archaic verbal use meaning 'to graze' (not the interrogative 'why'); خوار is a predicate adjective 'low/despised'; شد is the past tense copula 'became'; ارجمند ('esteemed') is the person being described, placed after the verb by poetic inversion — so literally 'the esteemed one became low/turned into a grazed/despised state.'
segments.s057-g51: یکایک is an adverb 'one by one / each and every' modifying the verb; همه ('all/everything') functions as the totalizing object (elliptically 'all things'); 'سام' is the subject; 'با او' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to/with him' (here 'to him'); and بگفت is the past-tense verb 'said/told' with the archaic b‑ prefix.
segments.s057-g52: The repeated هم ... هم ... is the correlative 'both ... and ...'. از آشکارا = 'from the open → openly' (آشکارا an adverb), and اندر نهفت = 'in the hidden → in secret' (اندر = 'in/within', نهفت = 'hidden'). Together: 'both openly and in secret.'
segments.s057-g53: ز + verbal noun افگندن (= 'the throwing/abandoning') forms an ablative/causal phrase 'by/through the casting away of Zal' (ز here marks the source/cause). بگشاد is used in a verbal sense 'opened/revealed' (poetic form), with راز 'secret' as its object: 'a secret was opened/revealed because of Zal's being cast away.'
segments.s057-g54: که introduces the content clause ('that/when'). چون here functions temporally/as ('when/as'), گشت is past 'became/turned.' با او is a dative/limiting phrase 'with/regarding him' (i.e. 'toward him' or 'in his case'), سپهر 'the sky/heavens' is the subject/actor, and از فراز 'from on high' (= 'from the heights') is an adverbial phrase. The clause says essentially 'that when the heavens turned (away) from on high toward him...'.
segments.s057-g55: سرانجام 'at last/in the end' is a sentence‑initial adverb; گیتی is the subject 'the world.' ز here is causal 'because of/on account of' introducing 'سیمرغ و زال' ('the Simorgh and Zal') as the cause/agent of what follows — a typical poetic ordering where the cause is placed before the verb.
segments.s057-g56: پر از داستان شد = 'became full of stories/tales' (پر 'full (of)' + از 'of' + داستان 'tale' + شد 'became'). به بسیار سال is a temporal/extent phrase 'over many years' where به marks the period/extent and بسیار سال means 'many years.' The whole means the world was filled with tales over a long span of years.
segments.s057-g177: گرانمایه (adjective) precedes the noun سیمرغ in poetic word order: 'the precious Simorgh.' برداشتش is the past-tense verb برداشت ('to lift/take') with the enclitic object pronoun -ش ('him/it'), so the clause reads 'the Simorgh lifted him.' The verb is simple past and agrees with سیمرغ as subject.
segments.s057-g178: همان is a demonstrative/intensifier 'the same' placed before the noun آفریننده ('Creator'). بگماشتش is a classical past form (بـ- prefix + گماشت) meaning 'appointed/placed' with the enclitic -ش 'him/it' as object, so 'that same Creator appointed/placed him.'
segments.s057-g179: بپرورد is the past form of پروردن ('to rear/nurture') with the classical b- prefix, meaning 'he reared/nurtured.' او را is the direct object ('him'), and چو سرو بلند is a simile 'like a tall/lofty cypress' modifying how he was reared; the simile follows the object in poetic inversion.
segments.s057-g180: This phrase contains two coordinated result clauses with the causative/caused meaning carried over from the verb of the previous clause: 'مرا خوار بد' = 'he made me despised' where مرا + را marks 'me' as object and خوار بد is the predicate 'despised/brought low.' 'مرغ را ارجمند' = '(he made) the bird esteemed' with مرغ را as object and ارجمند as predicate; the verb is omitted but understood from context.
segments.s057-g181: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when.' هنگام بخشایش literally 'the time/hour of forgiveness' is the temporal noun phrase, and آمد is the past-tense verb 'came/arrived.' فراز is an adverbial 'on high/above' placed after the clause for elevated style.
segments.s057-g182: جهاندار یزدان is a compound title 'the world-ruler God' as the subject. به من is the dative phrase 'to/for me,' داد is the simple past 'gave,' and باز follows as an adverb 'back/again,' so the clause means 'the sovereign God gave (him) back to me.' The object is understood from prior context.
segments.s057-g183: بدانید is an imperative (2pl) 'know/be aware.' کاین is a fused كَ + این meaning 'that this' (introducing reported content). زینهار is the noun 'warning/admonition,' and من است places possession with the copula 'is' ('is mine'). The clause therefore reads 'Know that this warning is mine.'
segments.s057-g184: به نزد شما is a prepositional phrase 'with you / in your presence / among you.' یادگار من است is a nominal clause 'is my memento' with من as possessor and است as the copula, so the whole clause says 'it is my keepsake with you.'
segments.s057-g41: This prepositional phrase stacks two location markers: بر 'on/upon' (preposition) + آن 'that' pointing to a distant/previously mentioned place, then برز 'height' (here a noun), followed by بالای 'above / atop' and another demonstrative آن + خوب‌چهر 'fair‑faced' (adjective/noun). Word order is poetic: the location is foregrounded before the verb. Together they mean 'on that high summit of the fair‑faced one.'
segments.s057-g42: تو گفتی که is a reported‑speech clause: تو (thou/you, poetic address) is the speaker, گفتی is the 2nd‑person past tense 'you said' (here reporting), and که introduces the content clause. آرام جان است و مهر is the reported predication: آرام 'calm/solace' modifies جان 'soul', followed by the copula است 'is' and coordination و + مهر 'and love/affection'.
segments.s057-g43: چنین گفت 'thus said' is a fixed poetic opening for reported speech. مر is an archaic weak object pronoun meaning 'me' (the person concerned), سام را is 'Sam' marked by را (here marking the addressee/indirect object), and شهریار is the subject 'the king'. The order is topical/poetic: manner (چنین) + speech verb + objects + subject.
segments.s057-g44: که introduces the content clause. از من 'from/of me' indicates source or concerning me; تو is the subject 'you' of the reported clause; این را is the direct object 'this' with را as the definite/object marker; به + زنهاردار gives the manner 'in an admonishing/strict way' (به here marks manner). Overall: 'that you got/received/are concerned with this from me, in admonition.'
segments.s057-g45: This clause uses the light preposition به plus خیره 'staring' (an adverb/participle) followed by میازارش, a verbal form made of the present/imperative stem میازار + ـش (him); here it functions as an instruction 'do not harass/torment him' when combined with the negative in the next clause. از هیچ روی is an idiom 'in no way / on no account' (از + هیچ + روی 'side/way').
segments.s057-g46: به کس 'toward anyone' + شادمانه 'joyfully' modifies مشو, the negative imperative 'do not become / do not be' (مشو = negative of شدن). جز introduces the exception 'except' and بدوی is an adjective/noun meaning 'original/primitive' (here idiomatic — roughly 'your own/original interest' or 'for yourself'). So the line commands: do not rejoice at anyone else; except for one's own/original concern.
segments.s057-g47: که introduces an explanatory clause. فر (farr) is the royal/divine glory possessed by کیان (the Kayanian kings); دارد is the present/past auxiliary 'has/possesses'. The conjunction و links the following noun phrase (چنگ شیر 'the lion's claw' or 'lion's grip') as another quality or emblem possessed.
segments.s057-g48: This continues the list of attributes: دل 'heart' (here in the collective sense) + هوشمندان 'of the wise' (an adjective/noun in plural) — 'the hearts of the wise' — coordinated with آهنگ 'intention / purpose' (not merely melody) + شیر 'lion' giving 'the lion's intent'. The phrase enumerates noble qualities and courage associated with the Kayan.
segments.s057-g121: و زان پس is a compound temporal phrase: و (conjunction) + زان (poetic 'from that') + پس ('after'), together meaning 'and after that'. منوچهر is the subject (proper name) and عهدی is the direct object placed before the verb (object-before-verb order is normal in Persian). نوشت is a simple past (3rd-person singular) meaning 'wrote' or 'inscribed'.
segments.s057-g122: سراسر here is an adjective/adverb meaning 'entire/throughout' modifying ستایش ('praise'); the phrase سراسر ستایش means 'consisting entirely of praise' or 'full of praise'. به سان is a fixed comparative expression ('به' + 'سان') meaning 'like/as', and بهشت is the comparison target 'paradise'. The whole unit describes the covenant's character.
segments.s057-g123: همه precedes the list and means 'all'/'the whole of'. The following words (کابل، زابل، مای، هند) are place names joined by و 'and'; the string names the territories included. The repeated و is simply the copulative 'and' joining the list.
segments.s057-g124: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. دریای is the ezāfe-like construct 'sea of …' where دریای + چین = 'the Sea of China'. تا introduces the limit 'as far as' and is followed by به + دریای سند 'to the Sea of Sind' (به here as a directional preposition 'to'). The phrase expresses the geographical extent: 'from the Sea of China up to the Sea of Sind.'
segments.s057-g125: ز (poetic 'from') introduces the starting place زابلستان. تا marks the extent 'until / up to'. بدَان is the fused form of به + آن ('to that'), here split in the segmentation: ب is the prefix/preposition be- and دان represents 'that' (classical/poetic form). روی بست is a verbal phrase: روی ('face/side/direction') + بست (past of بستن) meaning 'closed/bound' or idiomatically 'reached/was fixed on that direction' — contextually this marks a boundary/limit. Overall this clause gives a territorial limit.
segments.s057-g126: به نوی is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in a new way' / 'anew' (به + نوی), with به functioning as a prepositional/adverbial marker. نوشتند is past plural 'they wrote/inscribed'. عهدی is the object 'a covenant' and درست is an adjective meaning 'proper/correct', qualifying عهدی. The clause means 'they wrote a new, proper covenant.'
segments.s057-g127: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' (classical/poetic). این is the demonstrative 'this', and عهد و خلعت are two nouns joined by و: 'the covenant and the robe (of honour)'. بیاراستند is past plural 'they adorned/arranged/put in order' (bi- + آراستن), so the clause means 'when they had adorned/arranged this covenant and robe.'
segments.s057-g128: پس 'then' marks the sequence. اسپ is the object 'horse/steed'. جهان‌پهلوان is a title 'the world‑champion' and here identifies whose horse or the epithet attached to the requested steed; خواستند is past plural 'they asked/wished/desired'. The natural reading is 'then they requested the world‑champion's horse' (اسپ as the thing requested, جهان‌پهلوان qualifying whose).
segments.s057-g241: A coordinated noun phrase: ستاره‌شناسان (compound plural) + و + دین آوران. ستاره‌شناسان is a compound meaning people who study the stars; دین آوران is a plural nominal phrase 'bearers/bringers of religion' (i.e., priests/messengers of faith). The conjunction و simply links the two groups.
segments.s057-g242: Another coordinated noun phrase: سواران جنگی 'warlike/warrior horsemen' (جنگی is adjectival), followed by و and کین‌آوران 'those who bring vengeance'. The adjectives and compounds are post‑ or pre‑nominal in Persian but here appear as the modifier + noun sequence common in poetic diction.
segments.s057-g243: Literal order: 'شب و روز بودند با او به هم' — 'شب و روز' (night and day) is the time adverbial; بودند is the past plural of بودن 'to be' meaning 'they were'; با او 'with him'; به هم (an adverbial phrase) means 'together/constantly' here. The word order places the temporal phrase first for emphasis, then the copula + accompaniment.
segments.s057-g244: زدندی is an archaic past plural of زدن 'they struck/struck forth' used idiomatically with رای to mean 'they gave/struck (their) counsel'; همی is the poetic/habitual particle marking repeated or continuing action; رای بر بیش و کم = 'counsel regarding more and less' (بر marks the target/topic). Together it means they continually offered opinion about increases and decreases (i.e., matters of more or less).
segments.s057-g245: چنان گشت زال از بس آموختن = 'Zal became so/from such a state through too much learning.' چنان (so/thus) + گشت (past of گشتن 'became') indicates a result; از بس آموختن uses از + بس + the verbal noun آموختن 'learning' to express cause/degree ('because of/through excessive learning').
segments.s057-g246: تو گفتی is the second‑person past 'you said' (here the provided senses list for گفتی doesn't include the verb sense, so the sense index is unknown). The clause ستاره است از افروختن literally reads 'a star is from kindling' or 'is due to kindling/that which arouses' — the construction X است از Y attributes X as resulting from Y (از = 'from, because of'), and افروختن is used metaphorically ('to kindle / to arouse').
segments.s057-g247: به رای و به دانش are two parallel prepositional phrases ('by counsel and by learning'); به جایی رسید is a common idiom 'reached a place/station' or 'attained a position' (رسید past 'he reached/attained'). The repeated به marks the instrumental/means ('through/by').
segments.s057-g248: که introduces a result/subordinate clause 'such that'; چون خویشتن = 'like himself' (چون used for comparison 'like/as'); در جهان 'in the world'; کس ندید 'he saw no one' where کس normally means 'someone/anyone' but the negated verb ندید yields 'no one'. The clause describes the consequence: having attained that station, he found no one like himself.
segments.s057-g129: چو = temporal conjunction 'when'. این کرده شد is a passive/perfect-like clause 'this was done' (کرده = 'done' + شد as auxiliary/passive). سام is the subject that follows the subordinate clause; بر پای خاست is a verbal phrase 'rose to his feet / stood up' (بر + پای = onto his feet, خاست = arose). The whole segment means 'When this was done, Sam stood up.'
segments.s057-g130: که‌ای here introduces a direct address/exclamation (the classical sequence که ای ...), functioning like a vocative 'O ...'. مهربان مهتر is the addressee 'kind chief/elder' (two nouns in apposition). داد و راست are abstract nouns 'justice and right/verity' presented as the qualities Sam invokes or commands (an elliptical imperative/request).
segments.s057-g131: ز introduces source/cause 'from' (poetic). ماهی is a poetic use meaning 'moon-like one / beautiful face' (complimentary epithet). بر اندیشه is a light-verb construction literally 'upon thought' = 'fell into thought / pondered' (بر + اندیشه). تا here links to the following clause with a sense 'so that / as if / until', and چرخ ماه is a compound image 'the wheel/sky (and) the moon' meaning the heavenly sphere (the heavens). The segment sets up that the heavens were taken up with thought by the beloved's beauty.
segments.s057-g132: چو = 'when/as if'. تو شاه ننهاد is elliptical: تو ('you') + شاه ('king') with ننهاد (verb 'did not / never placed' here used in sense 'to set/place'), so the clause reports the thought 'that no king like you had placed...'. بر سر کلاه is the prepositional phrase 'on (his) head the hat' i.e. 'the crown on his head'. Word order is poetic and omits some auxiliaries; ننهاد is the main verb 'placed'.
segments.s057-g133: Repeated به introduces an instrumental list 'by/with': به مهر (by affection), به داد (by justice), به خوی و خرد (by disposition and wisdom). This is a paratactic enumeration of the virtues through which the addressee influences the world.
segments.s057-g134: زمانه is used metonymically for 'the age / the world / fortune' (here 'the world'). همی is an archaic continuous/iterative particle 'ever/always'. از تو = 'from you'. رامش برد is a verbal phrase 'took/received calm or solace' (رامش = tranquility; برد as 'took/obtained'). The clause says the world continually derived its calm from you.
segments.s057-g135: همه گنج گیتی is a noun phrase 'all the treasure of the world'. به چشم تو is a locative/experiential phrase 'in your eyes / to your sight' (prepositional phrase coming after the subject for emphasis). خوار is a predicative adjective 'despised / worthless', so the line means 'all the world's treasure is despised in your sight.'
segments.s057-g136: مبادا is an optative/negative particle 'may it not be / lest'. The sequence ز تو نام تو یادگار is elliptic and poetic: 'from you, your name [be] a memorial' — with مبادا negating it: 'may it not be that of you only your name remains as a memento.' نام here carries the sense 'name/reputation'. The line expresses the wish that the addressee's legacy be more than a mere name.
segments.s057-g113: This is a coordinated noun phrase naming objects made of gems: زبرجد (the gem) modifies طبق‌ها (plates/trays) and پیروزه modifies جام (cup). In this poetic word order the material or gemstone appears next to the object it qualifies; طبق‌ها is plural (‑ها). The conjunction و links the two item-phrases.
segments.s057-g114: The correlative construction چه ... و چه ... here means 'whether ... or ...'. از marks the material ("made of/of"); زر سرخ = 'gold (زر) described as red (سرخ)'; the second چه از ... خام gives the alternative material, with خام 'raw/unrefined' modifying the implied سیم (silver). This is a poetic listing of materials.
segments.s057-g115: پر از is a fixed adjectival/construction meaning 'full of / filled with' (پر + از + noun). The poet repeats پر to enumerate different scentings or fillings: 'full of musk and camphor, and full of saffron.' Each noun after از is the thing they are filled with.
segments.s057-g116: همه is the subject ('all' / all of them). پیش بردند is a past-tense verb phrase: پیش + بردند ('they carried/brought before') where پیش means 'in the presence of / before'. فرمان بران is an archaic/poetic locution indicating the addressee/place (the sovereign/command); together the clause means 'they all carried (them) before the ruler.'
segments.s057-g117: همان resumes the inventory ('the same' / 'likewise') and heads a coordinated list of defensive gear. جوشن is a cuirass/armor; ترگ and برگستوان are poetic/archaic item-names listed in coordination with و. The sequence follows a simple noun-and conjunction listing pattern common in Persian epic catalogues.
segments.s057-g118: Again همان introduces a second series, here offensive weapons. The nouns (نیزه, تیر, گرز) are coordinated by و. گران follows the series and qualifies the items (heavy/valuable); in Persian adjectives may appear after the noun in poetry to maintain rhythm.
segments.s057-g119: همان again resumes the list. تخت پیروزه = 'a throne of turquoise' (material adjective next to the object), and تاج زر = 'a crown of gold' (material noun following crown). The poem varies adjective/attribute placement for sound and meter but both are attributive constructions indicating material/ornamentation.
segments.s057-g120: This final noun phrase lists personal regalia: مهر یاقوت is 'a seal/amulet set with ruby' (مهر as seal/stamp or jewel) and زرین کمر is 'a golden girdle' (زرین 'golden' modifying کمر 'girdle/belt'). همان at the start links this pair back to the previous items.
segments.s057-g209: A compact noun phrase in apposition: ستاره (star) + شمر (a proper name) + مرد (man) + اخترگرای (compound epithet). The order places the generic noun 'ستاره' and the name 'شمر' together, followed by the descriptive title 'مرد اخترگرای' (‘a man who studies the stars’). This is typical epic apposition: name then a short epithet.
segments.s057-g210: چنین 'thus' sets the manner. زد is the classical past/simple verb 'zadan'; in poetry it often means 'befall', 'appoint' or 'strike' (here closer to 'befall/appoint'). تو را uses را as the definite/object marker attaching to the second-person pronoun. ز is the source-agent marker 'by/from'. اختر نیک رای is a noun cluster: اختر 'star' modified by نیک‌رای 'good/auspicious counsel' (نیک qualifies رای), meaning 'by the auspicious counsel of the star'. Word order is Subject (appositive NP) omitted here + چنین + verb + object (تو را) + source phrase (ز ...).
segments.s057-g211: که introduces a subordinate content clause 'that...'. ایدر 'here' marks the location. تو را again has را as object marker on 'you'. باشد is the third-person form of 'to be' used optatively/subjunctively in classical Persian ('may be' / 'will be' / 'is to be'); it functions as the predicate verb. آرامگاه is the nominal predicate 'resting-place, tomb'.
segments.s057-g212: A correlative pair using هم ... و هم ... meaning 'both ... and ...' with repeated ایدر for emphasis ('here both ... and here ...'). سپاه 'army/host' and کلاه 'hat/helmet' are the two coordinated nouns; the construction implies these (your army and your helmet) will be here — a terse poetic way to say both your troops and your gear lie here.
segments.s057-g213: گذر نیست is an existential negative 'there is no passage/escape'. بر governs حکَم here as 'against/from' (i.e. 'against the decree'). گردان is an adjectival/participial modifier 'turning' or 'revolving' modifying سپهر 'the sky/heavens', so حکم گردان سپهر = 'the decree of the turning heavens'. Word order places the negated existential first for emphasis.
segments.s057-g214: هم ایدر 'here too' is adverbial. بگسترد is the classical/poetic 3rd-singular (prefix بـ) of گستراندن 'to spread/extend' and here reads as 'will spread' or 'must be spread'. بایدت is 'it is necessary for you' (باید + enclitic ت 'to you'), indicating obligation/necessity. مهر here is a noun 'love/affection/favour' (contextually 'favour' or 'affection' rather than 'seal'). The clause says that favour/affection must also be extended here for you.
segments.s057-g215: کنون 'now' fronted for immediacy. گرد خویش اندر آور is an imperative/causative instruction built from گرد ('around') + خویش (own/one's own; here split into خوی + ش) + اندر آور ('bring in/bring toward'). Together it means 'bring (together) around yourself' or 'gather to yourself'. گروه is the object 'a group/company'.
segments.s057-g216: A simple coordinated list: سواران 'horsemen/riders' and مردان 'men' qualified by the compound دانش‌پژوه 'students/learners/scholars' (literally 'knowledge-seekers'). The nouns are the content of the 'company' just ordered to be gathered.
segments.s057-g25: چو = a temporal conjunction 'when' (archaic/poetic use). آمد is past tense 'came' (3rd singular). به + نزدیکی + شهریار = a directional prepositional phrase 'to/into the nearness of the king' with the possessor شهریار placed after نزدیکی (typical Persian genitive order).
segments.s057-g26: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed/commander'. پذیره here functions as 'received/accepted' (verbal/adjectival use). شدش is the past auxiliary/verb with the enclitic -ش referring to 'him' (the newcomer) so the phrase means 'the spahbed received him'. از کنار = 'at/from the side', a locational/ circumstantial phrase.
segments.s057-g27: درفش منوچهر is a genitive phrase 'Manuchehr's banner' (noun + owner after it). چون introduces a temporal clause 'when' (poetic/archaic use). دید is simple past 'saw', and سام is the subject (placed after the verb here by poetic inversion).
segments.s057-g28: پیاده شد = literally 'became on‑foot' → 'he dismounted' (پیاده used predicatively with شد). از باره is a prepositional phrase 'from the (saddle/load/pack)' (archaic usage meaning off his mount). بگذارد گام is a verbal phrase 'placed/put a step' (بگذارد = 'sets/places', گام = 'a step') = 'set his foot down/stepped forward.' Word order places the verb before its object, common in poetic diction.
segments.s057-g29: منوچهر فرمود = 'Manuchehr commanded/said'. تا introduces the subordinate/purpose clause here (meaning 'that' or 'so that'). برنشست is the verb 'be seated' (بر- prefix + نشست 'sat'); in this construction it expresses the intended action in the subordinate clause ('that he sit / be seated').
segments.s057-g30: مر is a dative/benefactive form 'to/for me' (i.e., 'beside me' or 'at my side' in this context). آن پاک‌دل = 'that pure‑hearted one' (demonstrative + adjective). گرد is used prepositionally as 'around/near' (so 'near me'). خسرو پرست is a compound 'Khosrow‑worshipper' or more generally 'king‑devotee' describing the person to be seated. The whole string = 'place that pure‑hearted, king‑worshipping man by/near me.'
segments.s057-g31: سوی = 'toward(s)'; تخت و ایوان = 'the throne and the iwan/porch' (two targets of orientation). نهادند is 3rd person plural past 'they placed/set' used idiomatically with روی ('face') to mean 'they turned (their) faces'. روی here = 'face/direction' so سوی ... روی نهادند = 'they turned their faces toward ...'.
segments.s057-g32: The correlative چه ... و چه ... = 'whether ... or ...'. دیهیم = 'rural/peasant' (a class of rustic people). دار and جوی are paired nouns forming two types of دیهیم: دار (literally 'having/house', here 'house‑holder/owner') and جوی (literally 'seeker/search', here 'one who seeks' — together meaning 'whether village‑owners or village‑seekers/poor villagers'). This is a compressed poetic pair distinguishing categories of rural folk.
segments.s057-g161: This clause has verb‑final word order typical of classical Persian: the direct object "جهاندیدگان" (the worldly‑wise) is marked by the object particle "را" and followed by the prepositional phrase "ز کشور" ('from the country/realm'). The verb "بخواند" is an archaic/narrative 3rd‑person form here meaning 'he called, summoned' (poetic past/epic usage).
segments.s057-g162: Noun phrase + verb: "سخن‌های بایسته" = 'fitting/necessary speeches' (sخن‌های plural + modifier). "چندی" is a quantifier meaning 'a few/several' and appears before the verb, and the verb "براند" (poetic narrative) here functions as 'send forth/utter/put forth' (used loosely as 'express' or 'offer'); the verb ends the clause in typical Persian order.
segments.s057-g163: "چنین" ('thus') sets up reported speech; "گفت" is the narrative past 'he said/spoke' (here used as a verb despite the provided noun senses), and "با" introduces the people being addressed ('to/with') — so "با نامور بخردان" means 'to the renowned wise men'. Word order places the adverb and verb before the addressed group for emphasis.
segments.s057-g164: "که‌ای" here functions as a vocative ('O ...') addressing the mobeds; "پاک و بیدار دل" is a coordinate adjective phrase ('pure and wakeful‑hearted') where "بیدار دل" is a compound adjective meaning 'with an awake/alert heart'. "موبدان" is the plural of mobed (Zoroastrian priests) and is the addressee of the vocative.
segments.s057-g165: "چنین است" is a formulaic copular phrase ('thus is / such is') introducing the content; "فرمان هشیار شاه" is a noun phrase 'the command of the alert/wise king' where "هشیار" modifies "شاه" and the genitive relationship (command belonging to the king) is expressed by placing "شاه" after "فرمان هشیار".
segments.s057-g166: The subordinating particle "که" introduces the content clause ('that ...'). "لشکر" is the subject 'the army.' "همی" is an old/poetic continuous/habitual marker attached before the verb, and "راند" is the narrative verb meaning 'drive/lead' (here 'make march' or 'put on the move'). "باید" expresses necessity/obligation ('must'), and the prepositional phrase "به راه" ('on the road / on the march') completes the clause.
segments.s057-g167: "سوی" is the preposition 'toward(s)' introducing destinations. The coordinated noun phrase "گرگساران و مازندران" names the targets: "گرگساران" is a hostile/wolf‑described place or people ('wolfish folk/brigands') and "مازندران" is the region Mazandaran; the coordination links the two destinations.
segments.s057-g168: Word order here shows a poetic inversion: the modal/auxiliary "خواهم" ('I will / I desire to') coexists with the verbal phrase "همی راند" ('will drive/lead continually') — in classical usage the auxiliary can follow or accompany the main verb for emphasis. "سپاهی گران" is the object 'a heavy/mighty army' (سپاهی = troops, گران = 'heavy/mighty'), so the whole means 'I will lead/drive a mighty army.'
segments.s057-g145: بسی is a quantifier 'much/a great deal' placed before the nouns. مشک و دینار is a coordinated noun phrase ('musk and dinars'). بر here functions as a prepositional/particle 'upon' attached to the verb; ریختند is the simple past 3rd plural of ریختن 'to pour', so the clause literally says 'they poured upon (them) much musk and dinars.' The object(s) precede the verb in normal Persian word order.
segments.s057-g146: بسی again quantifies زعفران و درم ('much saffron and dirhams'). و links the two nouns. بیختند is a past-form verb here (poetic/archaic variant of 'they poured/scattered'); in this dataset no matching lexical sense was provided, so it is treated as an archaic/prefixed past verb parallel to ریختند in the previous clause.
segments.s057-g147: یکی here works as an indefinite/article-like 'a certain' or 'a (single)'; شادمانی is the noun 'rejoicing/joy'. بد in classical poetic usage can be an intensifier 'greatly' (not the ordinary modern negative meaning); اندر is an archaic/prepositional form of 'in', and جهان is 'the world'. Together the phrase reads 'a (great) rejoicing in the world.'
segments.s057-g148: سراسر 'throughout' modifies the following locative expression. میان is 'among/between' and introduces the range 'کهان و مهان' — a paired poetic expression; کهان normally refers to the cosmos/heavens, while مهان is a poetic plural (here 'hosts/people/guests') and the combination means 'throughout heaven and its hosts/throughout the realms and people'. The word order 'سراسر میان ...' is typical poetic compression.
segments.s057-g149: هر آن‌جا که is a standard relative/locative 'wherever/whenever'. بد here is a short poetic element that does not carry its normal modern negative sense and functions to shade meaning (likely intensifying or linking); no matching sense is given. مهتری is an abstract noun 'preeminence/superiority' modifying نامجوی, which is an agentive adjective/noun 'name‑seeking' (one who seeks a name/fame). Together 'بد مهتری نامجوی' describes someone seeking fame/preeminence.
segments.s057-g150: ز گیتی is a poetic way of saying 'in/from the world' (ز used as a poetic locative 'in/regarding'). سوی سام 'toward Sam' gives the direction; بنهاد is the past of نهادن 'to place/set' used here in the idiom بنهاد روی = 'he set/turned (his) face', and روی is the noun 'face' (object of بنهاد). The clause therefore means 'he turned his face toward Sam.' (Note: بنهاد here is a classical past; in the provided sense list no exact match was available.)
segments.s057-g151: که introduces a wish/exhortation clause. فرخنده is an adjective 'auspicious/fortunate', and بادا is the optative particle 'may it be' (a fixed marker of a wish in classical Persian). پی here means 'following' (i.e., 'may [good] fortune follow'), and این جوان is the topical noun phrase 'this young man' (این + جوان). The sequence expresses a benediction: 'may it be auspicious; may favourable fortune follow this youth.'
segments.s057-g152: بر functions as a preposition here 'upon/for' or as a linking preposition in the benediction; این پاک‌دل نامور پهلوان is an appositive noun phrase 'this pure‑hearted, renowned champion' (پاک‌دل a compound adjective 'pure‑hearted', نامور 'renowned', پهلوان 'champion'). The whole segment identifies the person for whom the wish is intended.
segments.s057-g233: بیامد is a past tense verb 'came' (third person); the following phrase پر اندیشه is an adjective phrase 'full of thought' (پر + noun). دستان سام follows as a nominal phrase in genitive: poetically placed after the modifier, it identifies 'Sam' (سام) as the possessor of دستان; here دستان is read as a concrete noun tied to the person (see senses). Word order is verb + modifier + possessed noun + name, a poetic inversion where descriptive phrase follows the verb.
segments.s057-g234: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. The construction تا ... تا بود is an archaic/poetic way to mark duration 'as long as / until ... was'; here تا + چون + زید + تا + بود yields 'as long as Zayd was (alive)'. بود is the past copula 'was', and نیک‌نام is the predicative adjective 'well‑reputed'. Overall: 'who, for as long as Zayd existed, was well‑reputed.'
segments.s057-g235: نشست is the past tense verb 'sat / took a seat'. The prepositional pair از بر is an archaic poetic locative meaning roughly 'on/upon' (here governing تخت عاج). نامور qualifies تخت (adjective after preposition), and عاج specifies the material 'ivory' — together 'the renowned ivory throne'.
segments.s057-g236: به سر بر نهاد is a verbal phrase meaning 'placed on (his) head' — به سر = 'onto the head', بر نهاد = past 'put/placed upon'. آن فروزنده تاج is the direct object 'that shining (frouzendeh) crown' placed on the head; note the object follows the verb here (typical of epic poetic order).
segments.s057-g237: This is a terse, enumerative line listing equipment. ابا here is the noun (archaic spelling of عبا, 'cloak') — the provided lexical senses do not include this meaning, so sense is marked unknown. یاره is a companion/attendant in this list. و links items. گرزه گاو سر is a noun phrase 'a mace (گرزه) [with a] cow/bull head' where گاو سر is a compound describing the mace's head.
segments.s057-g238: Another short list: again ابا (archaic 'cloak') appears (sense not in the given list → -1). طوق زرین is 'a golden collar/necklace' (طوق = collar, زرین = golden). و links the next phrase, زرین کمر = 'golden girdle/belt' (زرین adjective repeated before کمر). The adjectives precede the nouns in these compounds.
segments.s057-g239: ز هر کشوری is a locative/source phrase 'from every country/land' (ز = 'from', هر کشوری = 'each land'). موبدان را is the definite object with را marking 'the mobeds (priests)'; بخواند is a past-tense verb here meaning 'he summoned / called'. The clause thus: 'He summoned the mobeds from every land.'
segments.s057-g240: پژوهید is past 'investigated / inquired (into)'; it continues the narration. The coordination هر کار و هر چیز lists the objects 'every affair and every thing' and راند is the past of 'to drive/expel' — here 'investigated every affair and drove everything away' (the exact referent of 'راند' is elliptical in the poem). Both verbs are third‑person past forms and form a sequence of actions.
segments.s057-g185: گرامیش دارید = literally 'you have him dear/esteemed': گرامی (dear, honoured) + enclitic -ش ('him') + دارید (present 'you have/hold'). Here دارید is used as an exhortation/instruction to the addressee ('hold him dear') and پندش دهید is a second imperative clause 'give his advice' with پندش ('his advice') the object and دهید the polite/plural imperative 'give'.
segments.s057-g186: همه راه و رای بلندش دهید is a hortative clause meaning 'make (or exalt) all his ways and his counsel'. همه راه و رای = 'all (his) ways and counsel'; بلندش دهید = بلند + enclitic -ش ('his/it') + دهید ('give/make'), here 'بلند کردن' used figuratively 'to elevate/honour'. The enclitic -ش refers back to the person’s ways/counsel; جمله is imperative/optative.
segments.s057-g187: سوی زال کرد آنگهی سام روی is an inverted classical order: سوی زال کرد = 'turned/took toward Zāl' (سوی = 'toward'), آنگهی = 'then/thereupon', سام = subject 'Sam', روی = 'face' (object). کرد is the past-tense verb 'made/did' used with روی as 'made (his) face turn', i.e. 'Sam turned his face toward Zāl.'
segments.s057-g188: که introduces a relative clause describing the person just mentioned: 'who ...'. داد و دهش گیر و آرام جوی is a sequence of predicates: داد ('gives' / gave) then a compound دهش‌گیر ('gift‑taker' or 'receiver of gifts', formed with دهش 'gift' + گیر 'taker'), و آرام جوی = 'and seeks peace' where جوی is the present stem of جستن 'to seek'. The clause characterises the addressee as both generous/receiving and desirous of calm.
segments.s057-g189: چنان دان = 'know/consider thus' — an imperative or hortative verb 'know this'. که introduces the content clause. زابلستان خان تست: زابلستان = the region Zabulistan; خان = 'khan' (lord); تست here is the archaic 2nd‑person copula 'thou art' ('you are'), so the clause means 'that you are the Khan of Zabulistan.' (Note: تست is an archaic copula form, not the modern senses listed in the lemma data.)
segments.s057-g190: جهان سر به سر = idiomatic 'the whole world' ('سر به سر' = 'entirely, throughout'). زیر فرمان تست = 'is under (your) command' where زیر فرمان = 'under the command (of)' and تست again is the archaic 'thou art' copula. The clause thus asserts that the entire world is subject to your rule.
segments.s057-g191: تو را = 'for you' / topical object with را used here to mark the person concerned. خان و مان is a paired nominal phrase (poetic) referring to the ruler’s domain/household/estates (خان 'khan/lord', مان is a companion word here used poetically for domain — the exact sense of مان is archaic/poetic). باید آبادتر = modal باید 'must/ought' + comparative آبادتر 'more prosperous', meaning 'should be more prosperous'.
segments.s057-g192: دل دوستداران تو شادتر is a verbless predication: 'the hearts of your admirers (are) happier.' دل = 'hearts', دوستداران تو = 'your admirers' (تو as possessive after the noun), and شادتر = comparative 'happier'. The copula is omitted, typical in Persian nominal sentences.
segments.s057-g217: بیاموز and بشنو are imperatives (commands) addressed to the listener: 'learn!' and 'listen!'. The coordinating و simply links the two imperatives. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' (classical z for modern az). هر دانشی is a genitive-like phrase 'every/each kind of knowledge' governed by ز.
segments.s057-g218: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause ('that/which' / 'so that'); یابی here is the subjunctive/imperative-form of یاب (yāb) and should be read as 'you may find' / 'that you find' (not a simple past).
