The following ز هر دانشی repeats the 'from/through every kind of knowledge' frame, and رامشی is the noun/result ('tameness, taming, or a thing that tames') that the verb yields.
segments.s057-g219: ز marks causes/reasons here ('from/on account of' — poetic usage). خورد is the substantive/infinitive sense 'eating/food' (not a present-tense verb). و ز بخشش pairs two causes: 'and from/for giving/donation.' میاسای is an instruction-form of آسا (to rest/cease) meaning roughly 'do not be at rest' (contextualized by هیچ to give a negative force: 'do not rest at all'). هیچ intensifies/negates (here 'at all' / 'not even a little').
segments.s057-g220: This clause is elliptical (typical of epic diction): the verbs 'gather/seek' and 'give' are implied. همه دانش = 'all (or every) knowledge' (direct object/goal to be sought). داد دادن is the verbal noun/compound meaning 'to give (gifts/justice/charity)' and بسیچ ('very/much') qualifies it: 'give much' or 'be liberal in giving.'
segments.s057-g221: بگفت این و برخاست is a compact narrative sequence: 'he said this and rose' (simple past narrative verbs). The following nominal phrase آوای کوس ('the sound/voice of the war-drum') begins the next image; its verb appears in the next segment (the drum-sound 'rose' / 'was raised').
segments.s057-g222: هوا قیرگون شد and زمین آبنوس use شد (became) in the simple past: 'the air became pitch-black' and 'the earth [became] ebony.' Classical Persian often places two predicative locutions in parataxis; the verb شد governs the first predicate explicitly and is understood to apply to the second as well. آبنوس here is a color/metaphor 'ebony-dark.'
segments.s057-g223: خروشیدن is an intransitive/verbal noun here meaning 'to roar/thunder' used impersonally: 'there was a roaring of ...'. زنگ (ringing/bells) names the instrument causing the roar; و links هندی, which qualifies the type (Indian instruments). درای in this passage is adjectival/qualific and in classical usage can mean 'possessing/characterized by' or describe a quality of the Indian instruments (poetic compression: 'Indian, rich/resounding').
segments.s057-g224: برآمد is a past tense intransitive 'issued forth, arose.' ز دهلیز پرده سرای is a prepositional phrase 'from the vestibule (دهلیز) of the curtained (پرده) chamber/room (سرای)' — classical Persian often strings genitives like this; the phrase as a whole indicates the source from which sound or people issued.
segments.s057-g201: گهی is an adverb meaning 'sometimes' placed at the start. زیر is the preposition 'under/beneath' governing the following noun phrase چنگال مرغ ('the bird's talon(s)'), where مرغ follows چنگال in a poetic genitive order. اندرون ('within/inside') is an adverb modifying the whole prepositional phrase and is placed after the noun phrase for emphasis.
segments.s057-g202: Two infinitival/verbal nouns are strung together: چمیدن (here used as an action 'to gush/fall') + به خاک ('to/onto the earth' where به has directional sense), then و linking to چریدن ('to graze/peck') with ز خون ('from/on blood') giving the vivid image. Both verbs are used nominally/infinitively to list misfortunes rather than as finite clauses.
segments.s057-g203: کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. دور ماندم is a predicate with دور (adjective/adverb 'distant') followed by ماندم, past first‑person singular of ماندن ('I remained/stayed'), so 'I remained distant'. ز پروردگار is a prepositional phrase 'from the Lord' marking source/separation.
segments.s057-g204: This line uses an inversion typical of Persian poetry: پروراند is the past‑tense verb 'nurtured/raised/treated' (3rd sg.), مرا is the object 'me' (here shown as the clitic split into م + را), and روزگار is the grammatical subject 'fate/time' placed after the verb for emphasis; چنین ('thus') modifies the verb. Put together: 'Fate thus reared me.' Note: the token labelled 'م' in the word list is the object morpheme here and did not match the provided senses.
segments.s057-g205: ز گل ('of/from the rose') is a prepositional/genitive phrase introducing the source; بهره من ('my share') is the noun + possessor. به جز ('except') introduces the exception phrase خار ('a thorn'). نیست closes the clause negating any other share: 'my portion from the rose is nothing except a thorn.' Note the poetic order: source + possession + exception + copula.
segments.s057-g206: بدین is a frozen/prepositional compound meaning 'by/with this' (introducing a consequence or state). با جهاندار پیگار نیست is a simple clause: با جهاندار ('with the sovereign/ruler') and پیگار ('battle/strife') as the noun, followed by نیست 'there is not' — 'there is no quarrel with the sovereign under this (state)'. Note: the element labelled دین in the word list does not match the usual listed senses and here functions as part of the demonstrative/compound.
segments.s057-g207: بدو is the dative 'to him'. گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (3rd‑person past), introducing reported speech. پرداختن دل is an infinitival phrase 'to give up/pay/give over the heart' (a poetic expression for surrender or sacrifice), and سزاست is an impersonal present 'it is deserved/appropriate', so the whole: 'He said to him, (that) giving up the heart is deserved.' Note: the provided senses list for گفت did not include the verbal reading, so that entry is marked -1.
segments.s057-g208: This is an imperative follow‑up: بپرداز is an imperative 'give/attend to/let go' (here 'surrender it'), و 'and' links the next command, بر گوی is an imperative phrase 'speak out/say' (بر adding emphasis/direction), هرچت 'whatever (you have)' and هواست 'your desire/whim is' completing the clause 'say whatever you desire.' The verb forms and imperative mood are typical of direct speech commands.
segments.s057-g225: This clause is arranged Subject + directional phrase + verb + object: 'سپهبد' (the spahbed) is the subject; 'سوی جنگ' is a prepositional/directional phrase 'toward battle'; 'بنهاد' is a past narrative verb meaning 'set/placed' (here 'set his face' or 'turned'); 'روی' is the noun 'face' functioning as the object of 'بنهاد'. The order is slightly poetic (verb before its noun-object element 'روی'), but the meaning is 'the spahbed turned his face toward battle.'
segments.s057-g226: This short noun phrase follows and completes the action: 'یکی' (= one/a single) quantifies 'لشکری' (an army/troops); 'ساخته' is a participial/past adjective 'made/formed'; and 'جنگجوی' ('warrior') qualifies the army as composed of fighters. Together: 'forming a single army of warriors.'
segments.s057-g227: 'بشد' here is the narrative/past verbal particle meaning roughly 'it happened / he went' used to advance the story; 'زال' is the subject (the hero Zal); 'با او' means 'with him'; 'دو منزل' uses منزل in the sense of a stage/stopping-place on a journey ('دو منزل' = two stages); and 'به راه' = 'on the road.' The clause therefore means 'Zal went with him two stages on the road.'
segments.s057-g228: This is a purpose/result clause introduced by the combination 'بدان تا': 'بدان' (literally 'to that/thereupon') + 'تا' marking purpose ('so that / in order that'); it introduces what follows. 'پدر' = 'the father'; 'چون' here means 'how' (introducing the manner); 'گذارد' is the present/subjunctive form of 'to place/arrange'; and 'سپاه' = 'army'. The clause means 'so that (they) might see how the father would arrange the army.'
segments.s057-g229: Word order: 'پدر' (the father) is subject, 'زال را' (Zal as definite object with 'را') is the direct object, and 'تنگ در برگرفت' is the verb phrase: 'تنگ' (tight/close) + 'در' (in/into) + 'برگرفت' (took up/ seized). The idiom 'تنگ در برگرفت' means 'took closely / embraced tightly.'
segments.s057-g230: This clause uses a noun + verbal noun + verb idiom: 'شگفتی' (astonishment/wonder) is the thing that acts; 'خروشیدن' is the infinitive/verbal noun 'to cry/roar'; and 'اندر گرفت' is a fixed combination meaning 'seized (him)' or 'took hold (of him)'. Together: 'astonishment seized him and he fell to crying/roaring.'
segments.s057-g231: 'بفرمود' is the polite/honorific past verb often translated 'he commanded/said'; it is followed by a subordinate clause introduced by 'تا' (here marking purpose/order). 'بازگردد' is the subjunctive/present-form verb 'that he return' (بازگشت), and 'ز راه' = 'from the road' (ز = poetic 'from'). The clause means 'he ordered that he turn back from the road.'
segments.s057-g232: 'شود' is the narrative/auxiliary 'become / let it be that' used here to indicate result or exhortation; 'شادمان' is 'glad/rejoicing' describing the manner; 'سوی تخت و کلاه' is a compound directional phrase meaning 'to the throne and (the) crown/headgear' (an idiom for returning to royal station). The whole clause conveys 'and (let him) go rejoicing to the throne and crown.'
segments.s057-g193: کلید is the subject ('key' used here in a figurative/possessional sense). در گنج‌ها is a genitive/locative modifier 'of the treasuries' (literally 'in the treasures') that qualifies کلید. پیش تو است is the predicate: پیش 'in the presence of / with' + تو 'you' + است (third‑person copula) = 'is with you'. The overall word order is subject → modifier → predicate, typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s057-g194: دلم here is دل + enclitic ـم = 'my heart' (possessive suffix). شاد و غمگین is a coordinated predicate ('glad and sorrowful'); the final است is the copula that attaches to the whole coordinate phrase. به کم بیش تو است is an adverbial/goal phrase: به indicates 'with/regarding', کم «little/scarcely» and بیش «more/greater» form a fixed poetic contrast 'sometimes less, sometimes more', followed by تو (you) and the copula است — i.e. 'my heart, glad and sorrowful, is toward you to a lesser or greater degree.'
segments.s057-g195: به سام is the indirect object/recipient ('to Sam') placed before the verb, آنگهی is an adverb 'then/thereupon', and گفت is the past tense verb 'said'. زال جوان is the speaking subject with an epithet ('Zal the young'); Persian poetry commonly orders the recipient/adverb before the verb and places descriptive epithets after the name.
segments.s057-g196: که introduces the reported clause. چون is the interrogative 'how' here. زیست is the lexical verb 'live' (an older form; the listed senses are absent, so marked -1) and خواهم is the first‑person auxiliary/modal meaning 'I will / I shall' (periphrastic future), so زیست خواهم = 'I shall live'. من is the explicit subject 'I' (poetic emphasis), and ایدر نوان = 'here (ایدر) youthful/young (نوان)' — together: 'How shall I live here as a youth?'. Word order is influenced by poetic inversion but follows the subordinate-question structure.
segments.s057-g197: جدا پیش‌تر are adverbials modifying location/time: جدا 'apart/separately' and پیش‌تر 'earlier/before'. زین is the direct object 'this saddle' (زین = saddle/trappings). کجا is the interrogative 'where', and داشتی is the second‑person past tense 'you had' (subject 'تو' is omitted). The clause literally asks 'Where had you earlier kept this saddle apart?'.
segments.s057-g198: مدارم is the main verb phrase in the first person singular (poetic/archaic usage; the exact dictionary senses given do not fit and so are marked -1); it takes a که‑clause as complement. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'. آمد is the past tense 'came', گه is a noun 'time/occasion', and آشتی is the noun 'reconciliation / truce' that follows گه (a poetic reversed order: 'the time of reconciliation'). Together the subordinate clause means 'that a time of reconciliation came', i.e. 'I suppose/think that a time of reconciliation has come.'
segments.s057-g199: کسی کو is an indefinite/relative phrase 'one who' (کسی = 'someone'; کو here functions as a relative/connector). ز مادر is the agent/origin 'from a mother' and گنه‌کار qualifies مادر: 'a sinful mother'. زاد is the past verb 'gave birth' / 'was born' (here used to indicate origin: 'who was born of a sinful mother'). The literal order is relative → origin phrase → verb, a common poetic structure.
segments.s057-g200: من آنم = 'I am that one' (من 'I' + آنم 'I am that/the one'). سزد‌گر is a conditional/optative particle or adjective meaning roughly 'if it is fitting/appropriate' (agentive reading possible in the lexical list; here it introduces what would be proper). بنالم is a subjunctive/present form 'let me lament / I should lament' (بنـ‑ prefix marks subjunctive/volitional in classical Persian), and ز داد = 'over justice' ('ز' = 'about/from/over', داد = 'justice'). Together: 'I am he who, if it be fitting, should lament over justice.'

sentences.s057-s51: For he was a renowned champion, lofty and alert, bold and a horseman.

sentences.s057-s80: They adorned robes to match their rank, and all of them sought the basis of superiority.

sentences.s057-s38: You have raised this servant — a reared bird — in humiliation and lamentation.
sentences.s057-s39: He will clothe himself in feathers instead of silk; his reward will be flesh at the time of the breast’s milk.
sentences.s057-s40: Do not burn my soul with your ill-will; grant me mercy again and kindle my heart.
sentences.s057-s85: Let this son remain with you; he is the counterpart of (my) life and the match of (my) heart.
sentences.s057-s86: Let my heart and soul remain here; my eyelashes will shed the blood of my heart.
sentences.s057-s87: In the time of youth, and when you brought (or accomplished) things, once I made a foolish judgment.

sentences.s057-s53: The king of the realm made a certain robe of honour, which everyone applauded him for.
sentences.s057-s54: From Arabian steeds, golden (saddles/accoutrements); from Indian swords, golden scabbards.
sentences.s057-s55: From dinars and furs, and from rubies and gold — many spreads (laid‑out goods) for me.
sentences.s057-s56: Byzantine servants in Rûm’s brocade — all his jewels adorned their bodies and his gold their cloth.
